textPlain: Hah, so my comment here spawned a post: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=45904434

ok, i dont get it.on my phone at typical distance and 90 scale i only see about an asprin tablet size area spinning. but at 180 scale i see almost everything spinning at same distance.i think peripheral vision is quite sensitive to movement/contrast changes, but the moving shapes have to be large enough to trigger those receptors?not sure what to conclude from this.
I don't get it, all I see is:"Bad request"am I missing the joke?
Does anyone know how the hell this works

Hi, I don't know what this is supposed to do, but I get pretty bad migraines and loading the page made me feel extremely strange almost immediately so I closed it.I would check to make sure this can't trigger migraines or seizures. Maybe it's just me, but also, please double check.
This is a truly incredible demo.

TL;DR it helps you identify the true diameter of your visual focus, which is said to shrink with old age (mine shrinks more in terms of _time_ dimension but that's a different issue!)For best results, use it _fullscreen_, change the #define `90` values to a higher value if you're on a high dpi screen.Stare at a few places on the screen and you'll get the effect of appearing to rotate only where you stare.It's pretty neat.
Amazing - but iPhone screen is too small. Works on my iPad.
Its supposed to show you how big of a radius your eye can focus on at a time, as we age the radius shrinks.Edit: seems like there isn't enough research to suggest the latter. Apologies
I don't know why you got downvoted. This seems like a very useful contribution.
180 worked pretty well on my Framework 16.
